input_text:
Launched from Cape Canaveral, Voyager 1 began its journey on September 5, 1977, followed sixteen days later by Voyager 2. This staggered launch schedule was in place because NASA had conceived distinct missions for the two spacecraft. The endeavor, propelled by a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a primary objective spanning five years: to study the outer planets, Jupiter and Saturn, during a rare astronomical alignment of these immense bodies, an occurrence that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe encountered Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it relayed the initial high-resolution imagery of the planet’s faint ring system and documented the energetic volcanism on its moon Io, which represented the first instance of such activity observed originating outside of Earth. Two years afterward, specifically on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest pass to Saturn, providing vivid records of its rings and capturing detailed close-ups of Titan. The flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart the plane of the solar system, thus concluding its planetary circuit while simultaneously commencing its passage into interstellar space.

Guided by a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 directed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, successfully capturing an image of our planet from a distance nearing 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the Sun's diffused brilliance, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This represented the last photograph taken by the spacecraft; afterward, the cameras were deactivated to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch copper phonograph disc clad in gold. This disc, compiled with contributions from a team including Sagan, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical pieces, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. The spacecraft remains powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators utilizing plutonium-238, whose 87.7-year half-life has gradually depleted the power supply.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is about 25 billion kilometers away from Earth, sustaining a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will reach the significant benchmark of having traveled one light-day.
output_text:
Launched from the site of Cape Canaveral, Voyager 1 commenced its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days later, due to the fact that NASA had devised separate objectives for the two probes. The mission, propelled by a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a five-year primary goal: to observe the outer planets, specifically Jupiter and Saturn, during a rare astronomical alignment of these massive bodies, an event that recurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe encountered Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the initial high-resolution images of the planet’s faint ring system and documented the energetic volcanism on its moon Io, which marked the first observation of such activity originating outside of Earth. Two years following this, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest approach to Saturn, successfully recording vivid details of its rings and obtaining detailed close-ups of Titan. This flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to leave the plane of the solar system, thus concluding its planetary circuit while simultaneously beginning its journey into interstellar space.

Guided by a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 directed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, successfully capturing an image of our planet from a distance nearing 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the Sun's diffused brilliance, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This was the last photograph taken by the spacecraft; subsequently, the cameras were shut down to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this milestone. It carries the Golden Record, a gold-plated, 12-inch copper phonograph disc. This disc, compiled with contributions including Sagan's, holds greetings in 55 languages, selected musical compositions, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. The spacecraft continues to be powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators using plutonium-238, whose power supply has been gradually depleted due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is approximately 25 billion kilometers distant from Earth, maintaining a speed close to 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will attain the significant milestone of having traveled one light-day.
